_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Acadia Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the rice prairie parish created in 1886 from St. Landry. Acadia is one of the youngest Acadiana parishes, meaning pre-1886 records reside in St. Landry Parish at Opelousas. Louisiana uses the Napoleonic Code tradition -- conveyance records, successions (not probate), and the parish Clerk of Court system.

current_courthouse.location: Crowley, LA -- 500 NE Court Circle. Art Deco-style courthouse built as a WPA project.

previous_courthouses.location: Crowley -- original frame courthouse built shortly after the parish was created and Crowley was founded by W.W. Duson as a railroad town
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a brick courthouse after outgrowing the original structure. Records transferred intact.

previous_courthouses.location: Crowley -- brick courthouse on the town square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current WPA-era courthouse. Records transferred intact to the new building.

record_losses.cause: Parish creation -- Acadia was carved from St. Landry Parish in 1886
records_survived: All pre-1886 records remain at St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas
record_losses.description: Acadia Parish was created in 1886 from the southwestern portion of St. Landry Parish. All land records, successions, and civil suits predating 1886 for the Acadia area remain at the St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court in Opelousas. Researchers tracing Acadia Parish families before 1886 must use St. Landry records. The original Spanish and French colonial records for this area are at the St. Landry Parish courthouse and at the Louisiana State Archives in Baton Rouge.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in what is now Acadia Parish before 1886, their land records, successions, and civil suits are at Opelousas in St. Landry Parish -- a different courthouse, a different town, and a different parish entirely.
record_losses.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

record_losses.cause: Hurricane -- August 1918 storm caused building damage and water intrusion
records_destroyed: some loose papers and early civil suit files suffered water damage
record_losses.description: The August 1918 hurricane caused roof damage to the 1902 courthouse, allowing water to reach some storage areas. Bound record books survived without significant damage, but loose papers in filing cabinets sustained water staining and some became illegible. The damage accelerated the push for a new courthouse, which was eventually built in 1940.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1918 storm damage was minor compared to the fundamental challenge of Acadia Parish research: the parish did not exist before 1886, and the rice-boom population that created it was largely new to the area, arriving from the Midwest and from other Acadiana parishes.
record_losses.source: Fontenot, Mary Alice, and Paul B. Freeland. Acadia Parish, Louisiana: A History to 1900. Baton Rouge: Claitor's, 1976.

deeds.completeness: Good -- continuous conveyance records from the parish creation date. Pre-1886 conveyances are at St. Landry Parish.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. The earliest conveyance books document the rapid land sales of the rice boom era, when Midwestern investors and Acadian farmers divided the prairie into rice farms.
deeds.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court, Crowley
deeds.online_access: Limited -- some indexes available through Louisiana Secretary of State Conveyance Records portal

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records continuous from parish creation. Louisiana uses 'successions' rather than 'probate' under the Napoleonic Code tradition.
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1886 successions are at St. Landry Parish. Early Acadia Parish successions reflect the rice boom economy -- succession inventories list rice land, milling equipment, and irrigation infrastructure.
wills_estates.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court, Crowley

marriages.completeness: Good -- civil marriage records from parish creation. Catholic parish sacramental records from Crowley churches supplement civil records from the 1880s.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1886 marriages are recorded at St. Landry or in Catholic parish sacramental registers at Opelousas, Grand Coteau, or other older churches.
marriages.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some marriage indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1886 court proceedings at St. Landry Parish. The early court minutes document the rapid settlement of property disputes as the rice industry transformed land use.
court_minutes.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court, Crowley

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- Louisiana statewide vital registration began in 1898 but compliance was uneven in rural areas through the 1920s. Church baptismal records are more complete than civil birth records for the early period.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans; Acadia Parish Health Unit

substitute_sources.description: All land records, successions, marriages, and court proceedings for the Acadia area before 1886 are at the St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court in Opelousas. This includes Spanish colonial records, French-language documents, and early American-period records.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas, LA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any Acadia Parish family before 1886. The St. Landry records include colonial-era land grants, early Acadian settlement documents, and the church records of the oldest Acadiana parishes.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.description: The Catholic churches of the Acadia Parish area -- particularly St. Michael the Archangel in Crowley (est. 1887), Our Lady of Lourdes in Rayne, and the older churches in neighboring parishes -- maintain baptism, marriage, and burial registers that supplement civil records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parish churches; Diocese of Lafayette archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Catholic sacramental records are often more complete than civil vital records in Acadiana. Baptismal records name godparents (parrains and marraines), revealing kinship networks that civil records do not document. Marriage records include parents' names and often places of origin.

substitute_sources.description: Donald J. Hebert's multi-volume compilation of church and civil records from southwestern Louisiana is the essential published source for Acadiana genealogy. The series abstracts baptisms, marriages, and burials from Catholic churches across the region.
substitute_sources.repository: Widely available in Louisiana libraries; some volumes digitized
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The single most important published source for Acadiana genealogy. Hebert compiled records from dozens of Catholic churches, cross-referencing families across parishes. For Acadian families, this series often provides the only accessible compilation of French-language sacramental records.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

narrative_hooks: Acadia Parish did not exist before 1886 -- {ancestor_name}'s pre-1886 records are at Opelousas in St. Landry Parish, a courthouse that served a territory stretching from the prairies to the piney woods.

The rice boom that created Acadia Parish also created its records -- {ancestor_name}'s earliest land documents likely describe the transformation of open prairie cattle range into irrigated rice fields.

Catholic church records in Acadia Parish name the parrains and marraines at every baptism -- godparents whose selection reveals the kinship alliances and community bonds that surrounded {ancestor_name}'s family.

If {ancestor_name} arrived in the Crowley area during the 1880s-1890s rice rush, they were part of a migration that mixed Cajun prairie families with Midwestern farmers who brought mechanical rice-farming expertise and capital.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Fontenot, Mary Alice, and Paul B. Freeland. Acadia Parish, Louisiana: A History to 1900. Baton Rouge: Claitor's, 1976.
Brasseaux, Carl A. Acadian to Cajun: Transformation of a People, 1803-1877. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.
USDA Soil Survey of Acadia Parish, Louisiana, 1959.
